questions.question: What is declaration merging in TypeScript?
options: A tool to combine multiple JavaScript files into one bundle
The compiler's ability to combine two or more separate declarations with the same name into a single definition
A way to merge two different git branches
A utility function to perform deep object merging
questions.answer: The compiler's ability to combine two or more separate declarations with the same name into a single definition
questions.explanation: If TypeScript sees two interfaces named 'User' in the same scope, it merges their properties into a single 'User' interface, rather than throwing an error.

questions.question: Which of the following TypeScript constructs supports declaration merging?
options: type aliases
interfaces
const variables
let variables
questions.explanation: Interfaces support declaration merging, which makes them highly extensible. Type aliases do not support merging; defining two types with the same name results in a 'Duplicate identifier' error.

questions.question: How do you add a custom property, like 'analytics', to the global browser 'window' object in TypeScript?
options: window.analytics = new Analytics();
interface Window { analytics: any; }
declare global { interface Window { analytics: Analytics; } }
module window { export analytics; }
questions.answer: declare global { interface Window { analytics: Analytics; } }
questions.explanation: To safely modify a global interface from a module file (a file with imports/exports), you must wrap the interface declaration inside 'declare global {}'.

questions.question: Why is module augmentation commonly used with frameworks like Express.js?
options: To compile Express faster
To add custom properties (like req.user from authentication middleware) to the built-in Request object types
To bypass security CORS checks
To convert Express to a frontend framework
questions.answer: To add custom properties (like req.user from authentication middleware) to the built-in Request object types
questions.explanation: Because third-party libraries define their own types, you cannot edit their source code. Module augmentation (declare module 'express') allows you to inject your custom properties into their types safely.

questions.question: Is it considered a best practice to use interface merging extensively in your own internal business models?
options: Yes, it makes files much shorter
Yes, it is the only way to achieve polymorphism
No, because finding the complete shape of an interface becomes difficult since it might be declared across many different files
No, because it disables strict type checking
questions.answer: No, because finding the complete shape of an interface becomes difficult since it might be declared across many different files
questions.explanation: While great for extending third-party libraries, scattering parts of an interface definition across your own codebase creates 'spooky action at a distance' making it very difficult for developers to understand the full shape of the object. Extending interfaces (interface A extends B) is much clearer.
